latest.title: Change in trading currency of Ordinary Shares
latest.content:
1 Apr 2026 07:00
RNS Number : 9093Y
Compass Group PLC
01 April 2026
Legal
Entity
Identifier (LEI) No. 2138008M6MH9OZ6U2T68
Change in trading currency of Compass Group's Ordinary Shares
Further to the announcement on 18 March 2026, Compass Group confirms that the trading currency of its Ordinary Shares on the London Stock Exchange (LSE) has changed from sterling penny (GBp) to US dollars (USD), with effect from today, 1 April 2026.
This transition aligns the Group's share price trading currency with its reporting currency, reducing FX volatility in the share price and simplifying the investment case for global investors. The change will not affect Compass Group's FTSE index inclusion or its LSE listing. Dividends will continue to be paid in GBP unless shareholders elect to receive them in USD.
Enquiries
Investors

latest.title: Total Voting Rights
latest.content:
1 Apr 2026 07:00
RNS Number : 0912Y
Compass Group PLC
01 April 2026
Legal Entity Identifier (LEI) No. 2138008M6MH9OZ6U2T68
COMPASS GROUP PLC
TOTAL VOTING RIGHTS AND CAPITAL AS AT 31 MARCH 2026
In accordance with its obligations under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules, Compass Group PLC confirms that as at 31 March 2026 its issued share capital consists of:
1,785,403,977 ordinary shares of 11 1/20 pence each of which:
1,700,442,139 ordinary shares of 11 1/20 pence each are admitted to trading and 84,961,838 ordinary shares of 11 1/20 pence each are held in treasury.
The total number of voting rights in respect of the ordinary shares admitted to trading is 1,700,442,139 with each ordinary share having one vote.
The voting rights of treasury shares are automatically suspended.
The total voting rights figure may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculation by which they will determine whether they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, Compass Group PLC under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.
For further information, contact:
Alison Yapp Tel: +44 (0)1932 573000

Change in trading currency of Compass Group's Ordinary Shares
As previously announced in its Q1 trading statement on 5 February 2026, Compass Group confirms it will change the trading currency of its Ordinary Shares on the London Stock Exchange (LSE) from sterling (GBP) to US dollars (USD), effective 1 April 2026.
This transition aligns the Group's share price trading currency with its reporting currency, reducing FX volatility in the share price and simplifying the investment case for global investors. The change will not affect Compass Group's FTSE index inclusion or its LSE listing. Dividends will continue to be paid in GBP unless shareholders elect to receive them in USD.

company_30s.what_they_do: CPG Ltd manufactures and markets household cleaning and personal‑care products in the UK and Europe. Their line includes eco‑friendly detergents, surface cleaners and hand soaps that are sold through supermarkets, pharmacies and online retailers.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The consumer‑packaged‑goods market is one of the most resilient sectors, with billions of pounds in annual sales. CPG’s focus on sustainable, mid‑price products gives it a niche against big incumbents, while its strong distribution network keeps it in the daily shopping lists of millions of households.
company_30s.current_state: The firm is still operating but has been hit hard by supply‑chain bottlenecks and a recent dip in consumer demand. Cash burn is moderate and the company is working to shore up its balance sheet. The stock is trading at a steep discount to its historic valuation, reflecting the market’s loss of confidence.

rns_narrative: The company has recently shifted its share denomination from sterling to US dollars, a structural change aimed at attracting a broader investor base and simplifying cross‑border trading.
Alongside the currency change, CPG has been updating its corporate governance—adjusting voting rights and shareholding disclosures—while publishing its latest quarterly and annual financials.
These announcements suggest the firm is normalising its reporting and governance rather than launching a new growth initiative, so no immediate catalyst is visible yet.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Confusion‑turned‑cautious optimism – traders are puzzled by the currency shift but some see a rebound if the stock recovers.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: A few voices, like Stevie02 and stargate, highlight a potential short‑term bounce driven by institutional buying once the market digests the currency change.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Skeptics point to the company’s weak earnings and the fact that the price is still a fraction of its 52‑week high, warning that the decline may continue until a clear turnaround plan emerges.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: Early chatter was dominated by confusion and negative tone; recent posts show a slight shift toward cautious optimism as some investors believe the currency change could unlock new capital.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends is flat, meaning the market is largely unaware of CPG’s situation—exactly what contrarians look for.

near_term_catalysts: The next quarterly earnings release (Q2 2026) – any sign of a turnaround in revenue or margin improvement could trigger a bounce.
A clear change in volume, such as a spike above the 20‑day moving‑average, would signal renewed investor interest.
If the share price breaks through the 0.00p support level (the lowest point of the recent crash) and consolidates for a week, that would be a technical confirmation of a bottom.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a 20‑day moving‑average cross‑up, a 10‑day volume spike, and any commentary from senior management about new product launches or cost‑cutting plans.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock is deep in the crash phase—capitulation appears complete. If the price holds above the 0.00p level and volume remains above average, mean reversion is likely to begin. A breakout above the 20‑day MA would confirm that the bottom is forming.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: The deep crash (over 70% drop) offers a classic asymmetric play: the downside risk is limited to the current price, while a rebound to the mid‑52‑week range would deliver 200–300% upside. The signal hierarchy suggests a high‑risk, high‑reward scenario typical of RED or GREEN crashes, and the lack of current catalysts means any positive news will be magnified.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: If the company fails to turn around its earnings or if the currency shift leads to liquidity problems, the stock could remain depressed. The worst‑case scenario is a further 10–15% decline before a recovery, but the market already priced in a large portion of that risk.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Risk: Current price (essentially zero exposure). Reward: Potential upside of 200–300% if the stock recovers to 50% of its pre‑crash level. Risk‑to‑reward ratio is effectively 1:4.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter when the price closes above the 0.00p support and volume is at least 25% above the 20‑day average. Use a 5% stop‑loss below entry and allocate 2% of your portfolio to this position.
playbook.if_watching: Hold a small paper position until the next earnings release or until you see a 10‑day volume spike. If the price stays below the 0.00p level for 30 days, consider tightening the stop to 2% below entry.
playbook.if_passing: No terminal risks (e.g., delisting) are currently visible, so the setup remains bullish if the price holds.

ai_insights.bottom_line: CPG’s deep plunge, dead market awareness, and lack of immediate catalysts make it an attractive contrarian play—once volume and a support breakout confirm the bottom, the upside is large while the downside is negligible.
